A downburst that encompassed the Surprise tornado this evening had a footprint that was approximately 10 miles long by 3 miles wide. A NWS storm survey estimated that wind speeds were around 80 mph with a maximum gust of possibly up to 100 mph which snapped several power poles.  In addition to the snapped poles the downburst winds flipped several center pivots, damaged trees, flattened crops and cause minor to moderate structual damage throughout a 28 square mile area.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the evening hours. These storms brought winds of 60 to 70 mph and rains of 1 to 3 inches. A few locations reported hailstones with the largest being the size of golf balls.  The storms started out near highway US 183 and moved east. The winds caused damage to trees, roofs and shingles in the Holdrege area. A Holdrege lumber supply company lost a large storage building to the wind.  Strong winds brought down power lines north of Shelton in Buffalo county. Outbuildings were damaged and large tree limbs were downed in Franklin county around the communities of Franklin and Macon. A center pivot irrigation system was overturned near Holstein in Adams county. By 10:30 pm CDT the storms had reached highway US 81 where winds of 60 mph caused more damage.  The outer walls collapsed at a home that was under construction in York. Damage to trees, roofs and a carport was caused by the storm in York.  Farther south, several trees were damaged or uprooted and some roof damage was reported in the Hebron area.  There was sporadic power outages noted across the region.  Corn and bean crops had limited damage from these storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of around 5 inches fell in the Fremont area from early Friday evening 9/15/06 into the early morning hours of 9/16/06.  The heavy rain filled ditches and caused street flooding in the normally poorly drained areas around town.  This included Airport Road in which flooding caused several vehicles to become stranded.  A basement of an elementary school also flooded when outside drains became clogged allowing water to pour in through basement windows.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph downed 3 to 4 inch diameter tree limbs.
Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree southeast of West Point.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down some 2 to 3 inch tree limbs northeast of Oakland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced penny to nickle sized hail along track.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph downed a 6 to 8 inch in diameter tree limb and a few smaller limbs southeast of Fort Calhoun.

A brief touchdown of a weak tornado was observed just north of Gretna.  Initially the tornado was reported near 180th and Harrison Streets, but a NWS storm survey put the touchdown in an open corn field about 2 miles to the southwest, near 204th and Giles.  Besides the broken corn stalks, no other damage was observed.  The Supercell thunderstorm that produced this tornado had a well developed wall cloud and a moderately long-lived funnel cloud that remained above the ground as it traveled over populated neighborhoods of northern Sarpy county and south central Douglas county.  The funnel was observed by numerous people and was caught on film by at least several storm chasers.  Darkness limited visually sharp images, but frequent lightning observed with the storm allowed for the numerous public and other reports of a funnel cloud.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 5 inches fell over a large part of central and western Saunders county during the overnight hours of 9/15/06 and early 9/16/06.  The heavy rain initially flooded tributaries of Wahoo Creek around the Wahoo area, including Sand Creek, flooding and eventually closing Highway 92 west of town, Highway 77 east of town and old Highway 77 south of town.  Besides flooding hundreds of acres of farmland, the flood waters also affected the Fairgrounds and surrounding areas of east and southeast Wahoo, and at least 1 car was flooded.  A dance floor east of town was severely damaged by the flood waters along with railroad tracks in the vicinity.  The water eventually consolidated into Wahoo Creek causing it to cross its 19 foot flood stage at Ithaca at 1215 pm cdt on 9/16/06.  The creek at Ithaca crested at 21.91 feet at 415 am cdt on 9/17/06 and fell below flood stage at 1245 pm on 9/17/06.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph downed some large tree limbs south of Pilger.

The storms continued to rumble to the east during the evening hours and dropped hail up to the size of golf balls and wind gusts around 70 mph. Large tree limbs were downed in Shelby and Fullerton. The severe weather delayed several area football games.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado did extensive tree damage and some structual damage throughout the town of Surprise including destroying a mobile home and tearing the roof off of a bar.  The tornado was embedded in a larger downburst area that did damage over a 28 square mile area surrounding Surprise according a NWS storm survey.  The tornado was rated an F2 in Surprise, where it initially touched down, and then weakened and lifted a few minutes later 1 mile northeast of town.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured on a home weather station anemometer.
The ASOS at Omaha Eppley measured a 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust.
A cooperative weather observer estimated thunderstorm winds of around 60 mph were responsible for downing several large tree limbs.

A lightning strike caused a fire and over $200,000 damage to a laundry facility near 20th and J Streets in Lincoln.  The lightning struck the roof next to the east wall and smoldered for several hours before being noticed.  Most of the damage was confined to the east side of the roof of the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the evening hours. These storms brought winds of 60 to 70 mph and rains of 1 to 3 inches. A few locations reported hailstones with the largest being the size of golf balls.  The storms started out near highway US 183 and moved east. The winds caused damage to trees, roofs and shingles in the Holdrege area. A Holdrege lumber supply company lost a large storage building to the wind.  Strong winds brought down power lines north of Shelton in Buffalo county. Outbuildings were damaged and large tree limbs were downed in Franklin county around the communities of Franklin and Macon. A center pivot irrigation system was overturned near Holstein in Adams county. By 10:30 pm CDT the storms had reached highway US 81 where winds of 60 mph caused more damage.  The outer walls collapsed at a home that was under construction in York. Damage to trees, roofs and a carport was caused by the storm in York.  Farther south, several trees were damaged or uprooted and some roof damage was reported in the Hebron area.  There was sporadic power outages noted across the region.  Corn and bean crops had limited damage from these storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 to 70 mph swept through the Wisner, Beemer and West Point area.  The winds downed a few trees and scattered large branches which caused some car, fence and home damage.  Crops were also flattened west of Wisner.

A microburst from a thunderstorm caused extensive damage to a small area from near Greenwood to the Ashland County Club southwest of Asland (which is near the border of Saunders and Cass counties).  The winds were estimated at around 100 mph by a damage survey compiled by a NWS storm survey team.  The most extensive damage was at the Nebraska Raceway Park near Greenwood.  Damage there included an overturned mobile home, camper, 4 empty semi trailers and a 2 ton storage trailer.  Two ticket booths were destroyed and significant damage was done to a set of bleachers and a crash fence.  Damage was estimated at nearly 1/2 million dollars at the track.  The winds also downed numerous limbs at the Ashland Country Club.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph downed at least 1 tree in town.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph downed multiple trees in town and snapped a sign off.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph split a large maple tree causing it to crush an SUV.  No one was in the vehicle at that time.
Heavy rains produced flash flooding and washouts across several county roads southwest of Wilber.

Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches fell over a large part of the upper reaches of the Big Blue River west through northwest of Crete and over the Turkey Creek basin near and west of Wilber during the evening and early morning hours of 8/16/06 and 08/17/06.  The heavy rains caused the Big Blue River to go out of its banks near Crete during the overnight hours on the 18th and caused Turkey Creek to go out of its banks by late morning on the 19th.  The Big Blue River at Crete remained above its 18 food flood stage until around 530 am cdt on 8/20/06, cresting at 20.68 feet at 830 am cdt on 8/19/06.  Turkey Creek first reached its 11 foot flood stage around 1100 am cdt on the 19th, crested at 12.64 feet at 930 am on the 21st before falling below flood stage around 330 pm cdt on the 21st.  The flooding along the Big Blue River and Turkey Creek affected mainly agricultural lowlands.

It was a very warm period for Thanksgiving with afternoon high temperatures in the 50s and 60s, a cold front moved into the area on Sunday and brought colder air to the region.  Occasional freezing drizzle developed across the region and produced some slick spots on area roads and bridges. This contributed to numerous car accidents, mainly on the major highways. One accident on Interstate 80 resulted in 5 indirect fatalities as the driver lost control of their pickup and crossed into an oncoming pickup.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle caused area roads to become slick in spots, especially bridges and overpasses. These conditions may have contributed to a fatal accident on Interstate 80 near the Shelton exit in Buffalo county. The driver of a westbound pickup lost control, cross the median, and collided with an eastbound pickup. Five people in the westbound pickup were killed and one was seriously injured. Two people in the eastbound pickup were injured and treated at a local hospital for multiple fractures.  Information from The Grand Island Independent newspaper.

A major winter storm spun its way onto the plains. With mild temperatures in place at the start of the storm, precipitation began as rain, and pretty much stayed as rain. In fact, freezing rain blanketed the majority of south central Nebraska on the night of the 19th and during the day on the 20th. By the end of the 20th, a fresh coat of ice resulted in broken tree limbs, power lines and power poles, especially along and west of U.S. Highway 281 across south central Nebraska. Some of the worst damage was west of Kearney into Dawson County where some towns didn���t have power for several days. Ironically, roads and sidewalks across the area were not terribly slick as air temperatures near 32 degrees and unseasonably warm ground temperatures combined to limit the surface icing. A small section of south central Nebraska measured 1 to 2 inches of snow toward the end of the event, primarily across Valley, Sherman, northwest Buffalo and northern Dawson counties.

A wide variety of significant winter weather began impacting portions of south central Nebraska during the day on Friday (12/29) and continued through Saturday night (12/30), before exiting the area on Sunday (12/31). Freezing rain, sleet, and snow began Friday afternoon in portions of Dawson county, with snowfall amounts as high as 9 inches. By Friday night most of the precipitation had turned to sleet and freezing rain, pressing further east to around highway 281.  This led to significant ice accumulations which continued through Saturday night. The areas just west of Grand Island and Hastings received significant ice accrual from the storm, with up to an inch of ice reported from Wood River and Juniata.  Many locations received at least one half inch of ice.  The deposits of ice have caused widespread tree damage, along with power outages, and damage to power lines and poles. |Snowfall reports up to Sunday morning showed the heaviest snowfall fell west of a line from Greeley to Alma.  Several small towns were running via  generators. The town of Holdrege was expecting to finally have the transmission lines repaired and switched from generator to commercial power on January 17.

A major winter storm spun its way onto the plains. With mild temperatures in place at the start of the storm, precipitation began as rain, and pretty much stayed as rain. In fact, freezing rain blanketed the majority of south central Nebraska on the night of the 19th and during the day on the 20th. By the end of the 20th, a fresh coat of ice resulted in broken tree limbs, power lines and power poles, especially along and west of U.S. Highway 281 across south central Nebraska. Some of the worst damage was west of Kearney into Dawson County where some towns didn���t have power for several days. Ironically, roads and sidewalks across the area were not terribly slick as air temperatures near 32 degrees and unseasonably warm ground temperatures combined to limit the surface icing. A small section of south central Nebraska measured 1 to 2 inches of snow toward the end of the event, primarily across Valley, Sherman, northwest Buffalo and northern Dawson counties.

A prolonged period of intermittent drizzle and rain fell across eastern Nebraska from Tuesday night, 12/19/06, through Thursday 12/21/06.  Most of the precipitation remained liquid, except across parts of northeast Nebraska where a period of freezing rain fell.  The area especially affected was southern Antelope county near Elgin and Petersburg where ice accumulated over 1/4 inch on trees and power lines.  This ice accumulation on the trees caused several of them to fall which downed power lines and caused damage to a few houses and vehicles.  The downed power lines resulted in some power outages, especially south of Highway 275.  Surface temperatures during much of the event hovered right around freezing.  With ground temperatures still relatively warm due to recent mild weather, roads, although slick at times, were not as affected.

Freezing drizzle and light freezing rain developed over sections of northeast Nebraska north of a stationary front that extended across far southeast Nebraska Sunday afternoon and evening 11/26/06.  The front separated temperatures that ranged from the 20s in northern Nebraska to the lower 60s across the far southeast part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and light freezing rain caused a 2 car accident south of Wayne as a northbound vehicle lost control and slid into the path of a southbound car. Injuries were apparently minor.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and light freezing rain developed over sections of northeast Nebraska north of a stationary front that extended across far southeast Nebraska Sunday afternoon and evening 11/26/06.  The front separated temperatures that ranged from the 20s in northern Nebraska to the lower 60s across the far southeast part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused several accidents around the Columbus area with a few vehicles sliding off rural roads, river bridges, and Highway 81 near town, including at least one that rolled over onto its side. Injuries were apparently minor in all cases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving winter storm produced widespread blizzard conditions over much of the central and southern Nebraska panhandle, causing some power outages and closing most roads.  More than 20 tractor-trailer trucks were reported to have been stranded along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and winds from 30 to 40 mph reported.  Estimated snowfall around 16 inches reported 15 miles south of Bushnell, Ne., with drifts up to 5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the southern rockies and moved slowly across Nebraska.  Due to the slow movement of the system, significant accumulations of freezing rain, sleet and snow occurred. Along and east of Highway 83, freezing rain began during the evening of December 19th, spreading northeastward and continuing throughout much of the day December 20th.  Ice accumulated from one quarter to one half inch causing downed power lines and numerous broken tree branches. Some residents lost power for up to 40 hours. Snow followed the freezing rain with accumulations ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Areas to the west of Highway 83 experienced blizzard conditions with heavy snowfall of 6 to 11 inches and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with total amounts of 8 to 10 inches combined with north winds of 35 to 40 mph on December 20th. This caused blowing and drifting snow with visibilities reduced significantly for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the southern rockies and into western and north central Nebraska on Friday, December 29th. Due to abundant moisture drawn in from the Gulf of Mexico, significant snow accumulations occurred. Some freezing rain occurred prior to a changeover to snow across portions of Boyd, Holt, Garfield, Wheeler, Frontier, and Custer counties where ice accumulations up to a quarter inch were observed. This caused some downed power lines and broken tree branches. Snow began to fall across western Nebraska during the early morning hours Friday and spread into eastern portions of north central Nebraska by late Friday morning. The snow continued Friday night before diminishing during the morning Saturday, December 30th. Snow accumulations of 6 to 13 inches combined with winds of 15 to 30 mph causing blowing and drifting snow.

Ice accumulated from one quarter to one half inch causing downed power lines and numerous broken tree branches. Some residents lost power for up to 40 hours. Snow followed the freezing rain with accumulations ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Areas to the west of Highway 83 experienced blizzard conditions with heavy snowfall of 6 to 11 inches and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch caused broken tree branches and snapped power lines. Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches followed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the southern rockies and into western and north central Nebraska on Friday, December 29th. Due to abundant moisture drawn in from the Gulf of Mexico, significant snow accumulations occurred. Some freezing rain occurred prior to a changeover to snow across portions of Boyd, Holt, Garfield, Wheeler, Frontier, and Custer counties where ice accumulations up to a quarter inch were observed. This caused some downed power lines and broken tree branches. Snow began to fall across western Nebraska during the early morning hours Friday and spread into eastern portions of north central Nebraska by late Friday morning. The snow continued Friday night before diminishing during the morning Saturday, December 30th. Snow accumulations of 6 to 13 inches combined with winds of 15 to 30 mph causing blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches.  The snow combined with 15 to 30 mph winds causing blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system moved out of the southwest United States and brought a mix of wintry precipitation to eastern Nebraska.  Ahead of the system, abundant moisture fed north into Nebraska with storm total rain amounts of 1 inch or more common over all of eastern Nebraska.  With temperatures below freezing for most of the event in sections of northeast Nebraska, much of this rain fell as freezing rain, except over the northern half of Knox county where the precipitation started as moderate snow.  The freezing rain mixed with sleet and snow briefly before ending over northeast Nebraska Sunday morning 12/31/06.  Farther south over east central and southeast Nebraska...the rain changed to a brief mix of freezing rain and sleet before changing to all snow by mid morning.|The freezing rain caused widespread major damage to trees and power poles and lines across a large part of central into northeast Nebraska.  NPPD, the power company that absorbed much of the damage from this storm, called it the worst in the company's history, a fact echoed by many people that lived across the area. In total over Nebraska, the ice storm knocked 37 main high-voltage transmission lines across 600 miles out of service, snapped or toppled more than 6,000 utility poles, including large steel structures, caused power outages for over 15,000 homes and businesses in over 30 communities and did an estimated $240 million in Utility damage alone.  Some towns remained without power for days afterward, and some more remote customers remained without power for weeks.  A major disaster declaration was issued for 57 counties in Nebraska from this storm.  As of late January 2007, the state was to receive $30 million, slightly more than the total received by the state for the previous 16 federal disaster declarations dating back to 1990.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulated between 1/4 inch and 1 inch in some locations on trees, power lines, vehicles and other untreated objects. The areas most hard hit in Platte county were to the west through north of Columbus. The weight of the ice, and a mix of light sleet that fell for a while Sunday, caused tree damage and also felled numerous power lines and power poles throughout the western and northern sections of the county. Around 500 power poles were snapped from near Monroe north toward Lindsay causing power outages in the region. Fallen trees also caused damage to cars and houses over the area most hard hit.  The storm caused around 3.2 million dollars worth of damage to utility companies in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system tracked across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and produced dime to nickel size hail over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

A strong cold front pushed into the area and brought the first freezing temperatures of the fall season to south-central Nebraska. Overnight temperatures fell into the 20s and low 30s. Readings ranged from 21 degrees at Hebron to 31 at Holdrege.  Ord bottomed out at 23.

A second, major winter storm within one week brought a large area of heavy snowfall to all of eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas. Two to three feet of snow fell in portions of east central Colorado and western Kansas, with snow totals ranking in the top ten all time highest storm totals for many reporting sites. Freezing rain also occurred over portions of northwest and north central Kansas with ice accumulations of 1/2-1, hampering clean-up efforts from the previous storm. The National Guard was called in to provide hay drops to stranded cattle. Interstate 70 and all area roadways were closed as a result of a combination of blowing and drifting snow and significant ice accumulations. Snow accumulations in southwest Nebraska counties included... Dundy: 8-15.  Hitchcock: 12-15.  Red Willow: 12-15.  Damage estimates are not yet available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals averaged 12-15 across the county. A woman was injured when her vehicle skidded on icy roads 6.5 miles south of McCook and rolled over.  Accident occurred at 9:45 am Friday, December 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system produced significant snow and ice accumulations across eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas.  Snow totals of 6-12 inches were common along the Kansas-Colorado border into extreme southwest Nebraska, with 1/2-11/2 inch ice accumulations in south-central Nebraska and north-central Kansas.  This storm produced widespread power outages and affected many counties with heavy tree damage and loss of utility poles. In SW Nebraska, Dundy county received 3-6 inches of snow on top of 1/4 inch of ice, Hitchcock county received 1-2 inches of snow on top of 3/4 inch of ice, and Red Willow county received up to 1 inch of ice with little or no snow accumulation.  Damage estimates are not yet available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow was reported, after freezing rain produced up to 1/4 inch of ice.

In fact, freezing rain blanketed the majority of south central Nebraska on the night of the 19th and during the day on the 20th. By the end of the 20th, a fresh coat of ice resulted in broken tree limbs, power lines and power poles, especially along and west of U.S. Highway 281 across south central Nebraska. Some of the worst damage was west of Kearney into Dawson County where some towns didn���t have power for several days. Ironically, roads and sidewalks across the area were not terribly slick as air temperatures near 32 degrees and unseasonably warm ground temperatures combined to limit the surface icing. A small section of south central Nebraska measured 1 to 2 inches of snow toward the end of the event, primarily across Valley, Sherman, northwest Buffalo and northern Dawson counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow set up across Lancaster county, with the southern sections of Lincoln the hardest hit.  Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches fell both just north and south of Lincoln, but sections of Lincoln, especially the south part of town, received 10 to 12 inches.  The precipitation started as a brief period of freezing rain, with heavy snow falling from around 8 am through 3 pm when it ended quickly as the band weakened and shifted east of the county.

Snowfall by mid Saturday morning had accumulated to a bit over 6 inches near Niobrara.  Meanwhile, freezing rain developed east through south of Niobrara Friday night with snow mixing with or changing to freezing rain over most of the county Saturday.  Some ice accumulations of between 1/4 inch and 1 inch occurred, mainly from around Crofton and Bloomfield toward Creighton then to Wausa.  The icing caused tree and power pole damage and which downed power lines knocking out power to sections of the county, mainly the southeast half.  Around 50 power poles were downed around Crofton.  The storm caused around 1/2 million dollars worth of damage to utility companies.

NPPD, the power company that absorbed much of the damage from this storm, called it the worst in the company's history, a fact echoed by many people that lived across the area. In total over Nebraska, the ice storm knocked 37 main high-voltage transmission lines across 600 miles out of service, snapped or toppled more than 6,000 utility poles, including large steel structures, caused power outages for over 15,000 homes and businesses in over 30 communities and did an estimated $240 million in Utility damage alone.  Some towns remained without power for days afterward, and some more remote customers remained without power for weeks.  A major disaster declaration was issued for 57 counties in Nebraska from this storm.  As of late January 2007, the state was to receive $30 million, slightly more than the total received by the state for the previous 16 federal disaster declarations dating back to 1990.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulated between 1/4 inch to around 1 inch over mostly the southern half of Cedar county on trees, power lines, vehicles and other untreated objects. The weight of the ice, and a mix of light sleet and snow that fell briefly late Saturday night, caused tree damage and also felled power lines and power poles mainly south of Hartington. The downed power lines caused some power outages over the southern part of Cedar county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm spun its way onto the plains. With mild temperatures in place at the start of the storm, precipitation began as rain, and pretty much stayed as rain. In fact, freezing rain blanketed the majority of south central Nebraska on the night of the 19th and during the day on the 20th. By the end of the 20th, a fresh coat of ice resulted in broken tree limbs, power lines and power poles, especially along and west of U.S. Highway 281 across south central Nebraska. Some of the worst damage was west of Kearney into Dawson County where some towns didn���t have power for several days. Ironically, roads and sidewalks across the area were not terribly slick as air temperatures near 32 degrees and unseasonably warm ground temperatures combined to limit the surface icing. A small section of south central Nebraska measured 1 to 2 inches of snow toward the end of the event, primarily across Valley, Sherman, northwest Buffalo and northern Dawson counties.

A large low pressure system moved out of the southwest United States and brought a mix of wintry precipitation to eastern Nebraska.  Ahead of the system, abundant moisture fed north into Nebraska with storm total rain amounts of 1 inch or more common over all of eastern Nebraska.  With temperatures below freezing for most of the event in sections of northeast Nebraska, much of this rain fell as freezing rain, except over the northern half of Knox county where the precipitation started as moderate snow. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulated between 1/4 inch and 1/2 inch in some locations on trees, power lines, vehicles and other untreated objects.  The weight of the ice, and a mix of light sleet or snow that fell for a while Sunday, caused tree and power line damage knocking power to parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wide variety of significant winter weather began impacting portions of south central Nebraska during the day on Friday (12/29) and continued through Saturday night (12/30), before exiting the area on Sunday (12/31). Freezing rain, sleet, and snow began Friday afternoon in portions of Dawson county, with snowfall amounts as high as 9 inches. By Friday night most of the precipitation had turned to sleet and freezing rain, pressing further east to around highway 281.  This led to significant ice accumulations which continued through Saturday night. The areas just west of Grand Island and Hastings received significant ice accrual from the storm, with up to an inch of ice reported from Wood River and Juniata.  Many locations received at least one half inch of ice.  The deposits of ice have caused widespread tree damage, along with power outages, and damage to power lines and poles. |Snowfall reports up to Sunday morning showed the heaviest snowfall fell west of a line from Greeley to Alma.  Several small towns were running via  generators. The town of Holdrege was expecting to finally have the transmission lines repaired and switched from generator to commercial power on January 17.

A strong cold front swept through the area producing 55 to 60 mph northwesterly winds over much of eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas. Roof damage occurred at a home in McCook, Nebraska.

A strong storm system moving across the central plains brought very strong northwesterly winds to portions of western Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Behind the cold front, a few locations reported wind damage and wind gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts in excess of 60 mph caused blowing dust and reduced visibilities on Interstate 80. This attributed to an accident reported 10 miles east of Chappell causing closure of Interstate 80 at mile marker 96.  The accident involved 3 semi trucks with 1 person treated and released with injury. The interstate was closed from 630 pm cst to 930 pm cst.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts at the Imperial, NE ASOS were measured at 60 mph at 748 pm cst.  At the same time, law enforcement reported a roof blown off in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms intensified along the Nebraska-Kansas border and moved ESE, producing hail up to half-dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail covered the ground.
